metadata.subject: English Language Arts
metadata.description: EFP Literary Studies 10 explores First Peoples oral and written literary traditions, examining how Indigenous authors use storytelling to convey worldviews, histories, and contemporary perspectives. Students engage with a diverse range of Indigenous writers and literary forms, including traditional narratives, contemporary fiction, poetry, and land-based narratives that reflect the cultural richness of First Peoples. Through close reading and literary analysis, students develop critical thinking skills while deepening their appreciation for Indigenous voices and narratives.
metadata.general_description: An exploration of First Peoples literary traditions and contemporary Indigenous authors through diverse literary forms and cultural perspectives.

big_ideas.statement: Literature serves as a vessel for preserving and sharing First Peoples knowledge, histories, and worldviews across generations.
big_ideas.elaboration: Indigenous literary traditions function as living repositories of cultural wisdom, connecting past teachings to present experiences and future generations. Through oral storytelling and written narratives, First Peoples literature sustains and transmits essential knowledge about land relationships, kinship systems, spiritual beliefs, and community values.

big_ideas.statement: Indigenous authors employ distinctive narrative techniques and literary forms rooted in traditional storytelling practices.
big_ideas.elaboration: First Peoples writers draw on oral tradition structures, cyclical time concepts, layered meanings, and collaborative storytelling methods to create literary works that authentically represent Indigenous worldviews. These techniques differ from Western literary conventions and offer readers unique perspectives on character, plot, and thematic development.

big_ideas.statement: Land relationships and environmental stewardship are central themes in First Peoples literature and shape narrative meaning.
big_ideas.elaboration: Indigenous literary works frequently emphasize reciprocal relationships with the natural world, responsiveness to seasonal cycles, and stewardship responsibilities. Land is often depicted as an active participant in narratives rather than a passive backdrop, reflecting foundational First Peoples philosophies about interconnection and balance.

big_ideas.statement: Contemporary Indigenous literature responds to historical trauma, resilience, and self-determination while celebrating cultural renewal and continuity.
big_ideas.elaboration: Modern First Peoples writers address colonialism, cultural suppression, and systemic injustices while simultaneously affirming Indigenous identity, strength, and cultural persistence. Literature becomes a site of resistance, healing, and assertion of Indigenous sovereignty and cultural pride.

categories.name: Comprehend and Connect
categories.raw: Students comprehend increasingly complex texts and make connections between texts, personal experiences, and broader themes.

competencies.raw: Recognize and analyze how Indigenous authors use distinctive literary techniques rooted in oral traditions
i_can_statements: I can identify narrative structures, cyclical patterns, and storytelling elements characteristic of First Peoples literary traditions
I can explain how traditional oral storytelling techniques are adapted in written Indigenous literature
I can trace how repetition, rhythm, and layered meanings function in Indigenous texts
I can compare oral and written versions of Indigenous stories and analyze their differences

competencies.raw: Analyze how Indigenous texts convey cultural values, worldviews, and relationships with land and community
i_can_statements: I can identify themes related to land stewardship, kinship, and reciprocal relationships in First Peoples literature
I can explain how Indigenous authors express worldviews that differ from Western perspectives
I can analyze character development in relation to community responsibilities and cultural values
I can discuss how Indigenous literature reflects and celebrates cultural identity and resilience

competencies.raw: Make meaningful connections between Indigenous literary texts and personal experiences, community contexts, and historical events
i_can_statements: I can relate themes in Indigenous literature to my own experiences and perspectives
I can connect literary content to historical events, cultural practices, and contemporary Indigenous issues
I can recognize how different Indigenous communities express distinct literary voices and traditions
I can reflect on how reading Indigenous literature shifts or deepens my understanding of First Peoples perspectives

categories.name: Create and Communicate
categories.raw: Students create and communicate their own understanding of Indigenous literary themes and perspectives through various forms.

competencies.raw: Create literary analyses that examine how Indigenous authors craft meaning through distinctive techniques and cultural references
i_can_statements: I can write analytical essays that examine symbolism, imagery, and narrative structure in Indigenous literature
I can use textual evidence to support claims about how authors convey cultural values and worldviews
I can analyze how Indigenous authors address themes of resilience, resistance, or cultural continuity
I can communicate my interpretations with awareness of the cultural context within which Indigenous literature is created

competencies.raw: Respond creatively to Indigenous literary texts through writing, storytelling, or artistic expression
i_can_statements: I can create written responses that reflect on themes and characters in Indigenous literature
I can write poems, short stories, or narratives inspired by First Peoples literary traditions
I can develop creative projects that engage with Indigenous storytelling forms and techniques
I can articulate how my creative responses demonstrate understanding of Indigenous literary elements and cultural contexts

competencies.raw: Present and discuss Indigenous literature with others, articulating informed perspectives and engaging respectfully with diverse interpretations
i_can_statements: I can discuss Indigenous literary texts in group settings, contributing informed analysis and respectful perspectives
I can present research on Indigenous authors and their works with appropriate cultural context and acknowledgment
I can engage with differing interpretations of Indigenous literature and articulate my own understanding
I can participate in literary discussions that honor Indigenous voices and perspectives

areas.name: First Peoples Oral and Written Traditions
areas.raw: Study of traditional Indigenous storytelling, oral narratives, creation stories, and how these traditions are maintained in contemporary literature
topics: Traditional oral narratives and storytelling protocols
Creation stories and their cultural significance
Oral tradition structures and narrative patterns
How oral traditions are preserved through written literature
Variations across different First Peoples cultures and regions
The role of community in maintaining and transmitting stories

areas.name: Indigenous Authors and Literary Voices
areas.raw: Exploration of diverse Indigenous writers from various First Peoples communities, examining their distinctive styles, themes, and contributions to literature
topics: Contemporary Indigenous novelists and their major works
Indigenous poets and poetry traditions
Autobiographical and memoir writing by First Peoples authors
Indigenous playwrights and dramatic narratives
Children's and young adult literature by Indigenous writers
Regional and community-specific Indigenous literary voices

areas.name: Land, Environment, and Relationships in Indigenous Literature
areas.raw: Analysis of how Indigenous texts portray relationships with land, environmental knowledge, and interconnectedness with natural world
topics: Land as character and active presence in Indigenous narratives
Environmental stewardship and responsibility themes
Seasonal cycles and cyclical narrative structures
Sacred geography and place-based knowledge in literature
Relationships between humans, animals, and plants
Responses to environmental change and climate in Indigenous writing

areas.name: Resilience, Identity, and Contemporary Indigenous Experience
areas.raw: Examination of how Indigenous literature addresses cultural identity, historical trauma, resilience, and contemporary First Peoples experiences
topics: Historical trauma and intergenerational healing in literature
Cultural pride and identity assertion in Indigenous narratives
Responses to colonialism and systemic oppression
Contemporary Indigenous youth and urban Indigenous experiences
Language preservation and reclamation in literature
Self-determination and Indigenous sovereignty themes
